raw_response: {"rating": 3.8, "lean": "Right", "explanation": "This is a news report that consistently frames a scientific body's findings through the lens of skepticism while platforming exclusively right-leaning and conservative sources. The author's own prose characterizes the NASEM report negatively and treats rollback of Obama-era environmental regulations as legitimate or beneficial, supporting a Right classification. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author's own framing describes the NASEM report's mental health findings as a move that 'cherry-picks data and stokes climate anxiety' — this is editorial characterization in the author's own voice, not a quotation, and it prejudges the scientific report before any evidence is presented; (2) Source selection is exclusively right-leaning or conservative: Cato Institute, Heritage Foundation, Heartland Institute, and a former Trump EPA chief of staff — no scientific, academic, or opposing-perspective sources are included to balance the critique of the NASEM report; (3) The author describes the 2009 Endangerment Finding as 'hastily assembled with potential political motivations' in their own connecting prose, framing settled regulatory history through a skeptical conservative lens; (4) The author notes approvingly in their own voice that rolling back the Endangerment Finding 'will lower costs and increase freedom for the consumer' — a characterization reflecting a right-leaning deregulatory value frame presented as likely fact; (5) The related article link headline 'Dems Finally Realizing Most Americans Don't Want Enormous Climate Agenda Imposed On Them' is an editorial choice by the author or editor and frames Democratic climate policy as unpopular overreach; (6) The congressional investigation into NASEM is introduced as a closing detail lending institutional legitimacy to skepticism of the scientific body, with no counterpoint offered."}

parsed_output.explanation: This is a news report that consistently frames a scientific body's findings through the lens of skepticism while platforming exclusively right-leaning and conservative sources. The author's own prose characterizes the NASEM report negatively and treats rollback of Obama-era environmental regulations as legitimate or beneficial, supporting a Right classification. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author's own framing describes the NASEM report's mental health findings as a move that 'cherry-picks data and stokes climate anxiety' — this is editorial characterization in the author's own voice, not a quotation, and it prejudges the scientific report before any evidence is presented; (2) Source selection is exclusively right-leaning or conservative: Cato Institute, Heritage Foundation, Heartland Institute, and a former Trump EPA chief of staff — no scientific, academic, or opposing-perspective sources are included to balance the critique of the NASEM report; (3) The author describes the 2009 Endangerment Finding as 'hastily assembled with potential political motivations' in their own connecting prose, framing settled regulatory history through a skeptical conservative lens; (4) The author notes approvingly in their own voice that rolling back the Endangerment Finding 'will lower costs and increase freedom for the consumer' — a characterization reflecting a right-leaning deregulatory value frame presented as likely fact; (5) The related article link headline 'Dems Finally Realizing Most Americans Don't Want Enormous Climate Agenda Imposed On Them' is an editorial choice by the author or editor and frames Democratic climate policy as unpopular overreach; (6) The congressional investigation into NASEM is introduced as a closing detail lending institutional legitimacy to skepticism of the scientific body, with no counterpoint offered.

explanation: This is a news report that consistently frames a scientific body's findings through the lens of skepticism while platforming exclusively right-leaning and conservative sources. The author's own prose characterizes the NASEM report negatively and treats rollback of Obama-era environmental regulations as legitimate or beneficial, supporting a Right classification. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author's own framing describes the NASEM report's mental health findings as a move that 'cherry-picks data and stokes climate anxiety' — this is editorial characterization in the author's own voice, not a quotation, and it prejudges the scientific report before any evidence is presented; (2) Source selection is exclusively right-leaning or conservative: Cato Institute, Heritage Foundation, Heartland Institute, and a former Trump EPA chief of staff — no scientific, academic, or opposing-perspective sources are included to balance the critique of the NASEM report; (3) The author describes the 2009 Endangerment Finding as 'hastily assembled with potential political motivations' in their own connecting prose, framing settled regulatory history through a skeptical conservative lens; (4) The author notes approvingly in their own voice that rolling back the Endangerment Finding 'will lower costs and increase freedom for the consumer' — a characterization reflecting a right-leaning deregulatory value frame presented as likely fact; (5) The related article link headline 'Dems Finally Realizing Most Americans Don't Want Enormous Climate Agenda Imposed On Them' is an editorial choice by the author or editor and frames Democratic climate policy as unpopular overreach; (6) The congressional investigation into NASEM is introduced as a closing detail lending institutional legitimacy to skepticism of the scientific body, with no counterpoint offered.
